metrics.narrative: The buyer never quantified savings or ROI; the articulated pain is qualitative: fully manual PDF-based vendor onboarding stored in SharePoint, ~100 vendors, and manual invoice review culminating in COO sign-off, all described by Heinrich Zaguirre on the 8 Jan 2026 discovery call. The only numbers exchanged were Zycus's own pricing ($80K-150K annual licensing, roughly a 1:1 implementation fee), which Heinrich said 'wouldn't be the number one driving factor' but would still be scrutinized by the AP team. No customer-side cost-of-inaction figure has been surfaced.

champion.narrative: Heinrich Zaguirre has done real internal legwork - he pulled together requirements, compiled and forwarded AP-team questions ahead of the demo, and organized the wider team (Anthony Fonzo, Denni Wong, Jasmine Shen) for the 8 May 2026 session. That is developing champion behavior. Set against it: he explicitly refused to name competitors 'to be fair to the AP guys' rather than help Zycus win, and he is the sole active thread - his vacation as of 19 Jun 2026 stalled the entire deal with no one covering for him. Engagement is currently cooling, not advancing.

competition.narrative: No competitor has been confirmed active by direct buyer evidence. Heinrich declined on 8 Jan 2026 to name rivals but confirmed MFAM is evaluating both Gartner Magic Quadrant leaders and smaller-scale platforms - consistent with the eight names logged in Competitors__c (Coupa, Oracle, SAP Ariba, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Workday, Ironclad), none of which has surfaced by name in either matched call. The real live threat to this deal is not a rival vendor but TransferMate, Zycus's own payment-execution partner, whose slow, rocky demo/pricing follow-through (8 May-26 Jun 2026) is souring the buyer against the whole Zycus proposal. The wedge that is landing is the Merlin/Teams chatbot, which Heinrich called unique in the space.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is well documented and specific: Heinrich Zaguirre described vendor onboarding as fully manual (PDF forms emailed, filled out, returned, then filed in SharePoint) with no self-service, ~100 active vendors and growing; invoices arrive via a shared inbox, are manually keyed, and route through a business-owner-then-COO approval chain; payments require manually building and uploading a file to BMO with no end-to-end automation (8 Jan 2026). On 8 May 2026, Denni Wong raised a related operational risk: suppliers may omit banking fields TransferMate needs to execute payment. Heinrich (IT) is the clearest pain owner, articulating pain on behalf of the AP/finance team.

economic_buyer.narrative: Heinrich Zaguirre named the approval chain on 8 Jan 2026 ('it goes to, like, our COO, for example... can you approve this amount'), and the Salesforce account record shows Jennifer Newman holding the Chief Operating Officer title, making her the most likely Economic Buyer. However, she has never joined a call, been named on an email, or been formally introduced to Zycus across either matched touchpoint (8 Jan, 8 May 2026). The EB is structurally identified but has zero direct engagement.

decision_process.narrative: The process so far: an intro/discovery call (8 Jan 2026) followed by a solutions-consultant pre-call and a tailored AP-team demo (8 May 2026), which pulled in a wider group (Anthony Fonzo, Denni Wong, Jasmine Shen) beyond the original single IT contact - itself a signal the evaluation is escalating internally. Heinrich named the commercial approval chain as running through the COO, but no formal buying-committee sign-off sequence or timeline has been shared. Progression has since stalled entirely behind the outstanding TransferMate pricing.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria have emerged across two calls: on 8 Jan 2026 the buyer prioritized centralized vendor/supplier data (vs. today's PDF-to-SharePoint process), invoice OCR automation, payment-file/remittance support, QuickBooks Online integration, and Microsoft Teams-embedded workflows (Heinrich called the Teams chatbot 'one thing that no other company does'). On the 8 May 2026 TransferMate demo, Denni Wong and the IT team probed onboarding-form validation, mandatory-field configuration, approval-workflow flexibility, and what happens if TransferMate cannot execute a payment. These are confirmed buyer-stated criteria, not inferred.

flags: No Economic Buyer (COO or CFO) has been identified by name or engaged, and without EB sponsorship this $100k annual SaaS decision cannot advance to budget approval or contract. Heinrich stated on 2026-01-08 that 'our COO' is the final approver for payment amounts, clearly identifying the COO role as budget authority. However, no COO has joined any call or been introduced, and the demo on 2026-05-08 was attended only by mid-level IT and Finance stakeholders (Heinrich, Anthony DiFonzo, Denni Wong, Shifa Garewal, Jasmine Shen). The immediate move is to surface the COO or CFO through Anthony DiFonzo or Denni Wong, position this as a strategic finance-automation and cash-flow initiative (not an IT project), and secure a 20-minute executive briefing within 14 days. Frame it as: 'We've validated technical and operational fit with your IT and AP teams; the next step is to align the business case and ROI model with your COO/CFO so we can move to commercial discussion.' Without EB engagement this deal will stall indefinitely at mid-level evaluation.

Single-threaded to Heinrich Zaguirre (IT Senior Manager), who is on vacation as of 2026-06-19 and has not replied since. No alternate buyer contact is actively engaged. Anthony DiFonzo (Director Finance & Operations) and Denni Wong (Director IT) attended the 2026-05-08 demo and are decision makers per demo notes, but neither has been developed as champion or re-engaged post-demo. Immediate action: multi-thread by reaching Anthony and Denni directly (not through Heinrich) with a brief update on TransferMate resolution and a request for a 20-minute call to confirm next steps and map the Economic Buyer. Position one of them as co-champion.

MFAM is evaluating Gartner Magic Quadrant leaders (Coupa, GEP, SAP Ariba, Oracle) and smaller-scale vendors, but Zycus has no visibility into competitive ranking, who else has demoed, what MFAM liked/disliked about competitors, or whether Zycus is on a shortlist. The deal is 170 days old with no down-select or finalist status communicated. The immediate move is to extract competitive intelligence from Heinrich (now on vacation, so pivot to Anthony DiFonzo or Denni Wong): ask directly 'Who else have you seen demo? What did you like about their approach? Where do we rank?' Then reposition with the Merlin AI wedge—Zycus's agentic automation, Microsoft Teams integration, and modern UX are differentiated vs Coupa/Ariba's legacy platforms, and the owner's manager Michael McCarthy (who spent 4 years at Coupa) can position against Coupa's complexity and cost. Make Merlin the centerpiece of the finalist pitch: 'The Gartner platforms you're evaluating were built 15-20 years ago and bolted AI on recently; Zycus Merlin is AI-native and embeds agentic automation into every workflow—supplier onboarding, invoice OCR, duplicate detection, and payment execution—so your 33-person AP and IT teams spend zero time on manual data entry.' This is the win strategy for a crowded Gartner-led evaluation.
Lead the next AP-team touchpoint with the Merlin/Teams chatbot - Heinrich called it something 'no other company does'; it is the clearest differentiator against an undisclosed Gartner-MQ field.

The single strongest current threat to this deal is not a rival vendor at all: it is TransferMate, Zycus's own payment-implementation partner, whose slow and rocky demo/pricing execution (8 May-26 Jun 2026) is actively souring the buyer's view of Zycus.
